decoded.literal: Perform a shelf-read in the 800s (Literature) section and return any out-of-order books to their correct position.
decoded.subtext: The 800s section has likely been browsed heavily and books may be out of Dewey Decimal order. The manager wants it neat for patrons.
decoded.why: In library vocabulary, 'tidy' means shelf-read. The 800s are the Literature section in Dewey Decimal. No deadline was implied, so this is routine maintenance at your own pace.

phrase: We have a hold that needs pulling before we close.

decoded.literal: Locate the reserved book by its call number and place it on the holds shelf before the library closes today.
decoded.subtext: A patron may arrive to collect this book today. If it is not ready, they will have wasted a trip. This is time-sensitive.
decoded.why: The phrase 'before we close' sets an explicit deadline. Holds that are not pulled on time reflect poorly on service and may require an apology to the patron.

phrase: The nonfiction section looks rough — can you do a shelf-read?

decoded.literal: The nonfiction section (Dewey 000–999) is visibly disorganised. Please check every shelf in sequence and correct any out-of-order books.
decoded.subtext: The manager has already noticed the problem, meaning it is obvious to patrons too. They want it fixed today but are not panicked.
decoded.why: Saying a section 'looks rough' is the manager's polite way of flagging a visible problem without blaming you. It implies the task is somewhat urgent — more so than routine maintenance — but not a crisis.

phrase: When you get a chance, can you weed the 600s?

decoded.literal: When your current tasks are done, review the 600s (Technology/Applied Sciences) section and flag old, damaged, or rarely-borrowed books for possible removal.
decoded.subtext: This is a low-priority background task. The manager is giving you the discretion to fit it in around your workload.
decoded.why: 'When you get a chance' is a social phrase meaning the task is not urgent. Weeding is a careful, deliberate process — the manager expects you to use your judgment about which books to flag and is not expecting immediate results.

phrase: Can you check if we have any withdrawn items that need to come off the floor?

decoded.literal: Walk through the stacks and look for books stamped WITHDRAWN that are still on the open shelves, then remove them.
decoded.subtext: Withdrawn books on the floor can confuse patrons and cause inventory errors. The manager wants a quality-control sweep.
decoded.why: Withdrawn items on open shelves can be accidentally checked out, causing system discrepancies and patron frustration. This is a data-accuracy and customer-experience task, not just tidying.

phrase: Can you get through the returns cart? It's pretty backed up.

decoded.literal: Sort and re-shelve the books on the returns cart, which currently has a large backlog.
decoded.subtext: A backed-up cart means the shelves are thin on popular titles patrons want to browse. The manager wants the collection accessible again.
decoded.why: 'Pretty backed up' signals the backlog is larger than usual. Sorting and shelving returns is a visible, patron-facing task — a cleared cart signals the library is well-managed.
